coredata.dc:title: Enhanced coagulation by high-frequency ultrasound in Microcystis aeruginosa-laden water: Strategies and mechanisms
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yitao
Shi, Xingdong
Zhang, Zhi
Peng, Yazhou
coredata.dc:description: Ultrasonic treatment has attracted much attention because of its physical and chemical effects that are distinct from those of chemical agents. In particularly, high-frequency ultrasound is known as an effective method because the theoretical resonance frequency of the gas vesicles in Microcystis aeruginosa is in the high frequency range (>100 kHz), which causes gas vesicles collapse and changes the settleability of the algal cells. In this work, the effects of the ultrasonic frequency, acoustic power density and duration on enhancing coagulation to remove turbidity in algae-laden water were studied. In order to explain the mechanism, the morphology of algae cells, the changes in extracellular organic substances, the zeta potential and the formation of hydroxyl radicals were analyzed systematically. Finally, Zeta potentials and flocs morphology after adding PAC were investigated to verify the mechanism. The results showed that the frequency exhibited fewer effects than power and duration on coagulation. SEM images showed that there were more severe cellular damages at 430 and 740 kHz than other frequencies. Sonication could cause the collapse of gas vesicle inside the cell, which was due to the instantaneous high pressure generated by the ultrasonic cavitation instead of the resonance. Furthermore, sonication would result in an increase in proteins in extracellular organic matter (EOM) with continuous ultrasonic irradiation, indicating that a small amount of proteins could promote coagulation and that the accumulation of proteins would inhibit coagulation. Free radical content testing showed that the production of excessive free radicals was often accompanied by a deterioration of the coagulation. The proper mechanical effects were the main mechanism of ultrasonic enhanced coagulation. Thus, it was recommended that the appropriate ultrasonic condition was the one that resulted in a small amount of protein leakage and little generation of free radicals, which occurred at 740 kHz and 0.02 W/mL in approximately 5 min, and would significantly enhance the turbidity removal rate in algae-containing water from approximately 80–90%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Enhanced coagulation
High-frequency ultrasound
Protein leakage
Gas vesicle

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Composition and content changes of AOM
Changes of zeta potential and flocs after coagulation

This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( No. 51778082 ), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( No. 2017CDJZR ) and Social Work and People’s Livelihood Security Science and Technology Innovation Special Project of Chongqing (No. cstc2017shmsA20019).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Ultrasonic apparatus at 120 kHz, 430 kHz, 740 kHz and 1.12 MHz.

Δ N R
in different frequencies and powers (60 s). The error bar indicates the standard deviation of double test.
ΔNR (%) in different frequencies and time: (a) 120 kHz; (b) 430 kHz; (c) 740 kHz; (d) 1.12 MHz. The error bar indicates the standard deviation of twice measurement.

SEM micrographs of morphology of M. aeruginosa cells: (a) cells before sonication (×5.00 k), (b) single cell before sonication (×30.00 k), (c) cells after sonication of 120 kHz and 0.09 W/mL for 5 min (×5.00 k), (d) single cell after sonication of 120 kHz and 0.09 W/mL for 5 min (×20.00 k), (e) cells after sonication of 430 kHz and 0.09 W/mL for 5 min (×5.00 k), (f) single cell after sonication of 430 kHz and 0.09 W/mL for 5 min (×20.00 k), (g) cells after sonication of 740 kHz and 0.09 W/mL for 5 min (×5.00 k), (h) single cell after sonication of 740 kHz and 0.09 W/mL for 5 min (×30.00 k), (i) cells after sonication of 1120 kHz and 0.09 W/mL for 5 min (×5.00 k), (j) single cell after sonication of 1120 kHz and 0.09 W/mL for 5 min (×20.00 k).
Instantaneous turbidity of suspension after sonication at different frequency, power and time: (a) 120 kHz; (b) 430 kHz; (c) 740 kHz; (d) 1120 kHz.

The fluorescence EEM spectra of AOM: (a) EOM of original suspension; (b) IOM of original suspension; (b’) IOM of original suspension at Ex/Em wavelengths of 200–500 nm/200–550 nm; (c) EOM of suspension after sonication for 1 min; (d) IOM of suspension after sonicatin for 1 min; (d’) IOM of suspension after sonication for 1 min at Ex/Em wavelengths of 200–500 nm/200–550 nm; (e) EOM of suspension after sonication for 10 min; (f) IOM of suspension after sonication for 10 min; (f’) IOM of suspension after sonication for 10 min at Ex/Em wavelengths of 200–500 nm/200–550 nm;
Effects of sonication on total soluble proteins and polysaccharides in EOM (740 kHz).
Effects of sonication on zeta potential of algal suspension.

Effects of sonication on zeta potential of algal suspension during coagulation: (a) algal suspension sonicated at 120 kHz and 0.02 W/mL, (b) algal suspension sonicated at 430 kHz and 0.02 W/mL, (c) algal suspension sonicated at 740 kHz and 0.02 W/mL, (d) algal suspension sonicated at 1220 kHz and 0.02 W/mL.

Flocs photos after coagulation: (a) flocs without sonication (×6.00 k), (b) flocs without sonication (×20.00 k), (c) flocs with sonication of 740 kHz and 0.02 W/mL for 5 min (×6.00 k), (d) flocs with sonication of 740 kHz and 0.02 W/mL for 5 min (×20.00 k).
DOC and SUVA after sonication at 740 kHz.
Production of hydroxyl radicals after sonication at 740 kHz.

head.ce:title: Enhanced coagulation by high-frequency ultrasound in Microcystis aeruginosa-laden water: Strategies and mechanisms

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Three Gorges Reservoir Region’s Eco-Environment, Ministry of Education, Chongqing University, Chongqing 400045, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Three Gorges Reservoir Region’s Eco-Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Urban Construction and Environment Engineering, Chongqing University, No. 174 Shazhengjie, Shapingba District, Chongqing 400045, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Urban Construction and Environment Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Three Gorges Reservoir Region’s Eco-Environment, Ministry of Education, Chongqing University, Chongqing 400045, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Three Gorges Reservoir Region’s Eco-Environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrasonic treatment has attracted much attention because of its physical and chemical effects that are distinct from those of chemical agents. In particularly, high-frequency ultrasound is known as an effective method because the theoretical resonance frequency of the gas vesicles in Microcystis aeruginosa is in the high frequency range (>100 kHz), which causes gas vesicles collapse and changes the settleability of the algal cells. In this work, the effects of the ultrasonic frequency, acoustic power density and duration on enhancing coagulation to remove turbidity in algae-laden water were studied. In order to explain the mechanism, the morphology of algae cells, the changes in extracellular organic substances, the zeta potential and the formation of hydroxyl radicals were analyzed systematically. Finally, Zeta potentials and flocs morphology after adding PAC were investigated to verify the mechanism. The results showed that the frequency exhibited fewer effects than power and duration on coagulation. SEM images showed that there were more severe cellular damages at 430 and 740 kHz than other frequencies. Sonication could cause the collapse of gas vesicle inside the cell, which was due to the instantaneous high pressure generated by the ultrasonic cavitation instead of the resonance. Furthermore, sonication would result in an increase in proteins in extracellular organic matter (EOM) with continuous ultrasonic irradiation, indicating that a small amount of proteins could promote coagulation and that the accumulation of proteins would inhibit coagulation. Free radical content testing showed that the production of excessive free radicals was often accompanied by a deterioration of the coagulation. The proper mechanical effects were the main mechanism of ultrasonic enhanced coagulation. Thus, it was recommended that the appropriate ultrasonic condition was the one that resulted in a small amount of protein leakage and little generation of free radicals, which occurred at 740 kHz and 0.02 W/mL in approximately 5 min, and would significantly enhance the turbidity removal rate in algae-containing water from approximately 80–90%.

ce:section.ce:para: Algae blooms have always been an ecological problem that has plagued humans in recent years. In particular, the occurrence of algae blooms in source water would threaten the safety of drinking water and the stability of drinking water treatment plants. On the one hand, the conventional processes, coagulation and sedimentation, adopted by most drinking water treatment plants do not remove these algae effectively, which will increase the coagulant level and clog filters [1]. On the other hand, these algae may release toxins, as well as taste and odor compounds [2–4]. Some studies have found that algae organic matter (AOM) is one of the most important precursors of disinfection byproducts (DBPs) in drinking water [5–7].

The difficulties in the treatment of algae-laden water are mainly in the following two aspects. 1). The negative potential on the membrane of algal cells [8] will increase the demand for coagulant and increase the cost of chemicals in water plants. 2) Gas vacuoles inside certain species may interfere with the sedimentation process [9]. Although it has been reported that these species can be removed effectively by dissolved air flotation [10], it is not easy for water plant using traditional sedimentation process transforms into an air floatation tank. Thus, considerable work so far is focused on pretreatment processes such as, chlorine, ozone, permanganate, activated persulfate (PS), and UV radiation [11–13], which have been reported to improve the removal of cyanobacterial cells. However, inappropriate methods may bring unexpected risks, such as releasing intracellular organic matter (IOM) or inducing algae cell lysis, which would cause greater interference with the subsequent processes and even threaten the security of the water quality.

Responding to cyanobacteria blooms, sonication as an environmentally friendly method has been studied, especially in drinking water treatment processes. Due to its physical effects, sonication exhibits better abilities for algae-laden water treatment than chemical pretreatment. Studies have confirmed that sonication can significantly enhance algal coagulation and achieve the same level of algal cell removal at low coagulant levels, which decreases the coagulant usage [14]. Ultrasound can also cause the gas vacuoles in algal cell to collapse, which improves the settling performance of algae [15]. In addition, the physiological activities of cells can be affected by ultrasound such as the disruption of photosynthesis due to the destruction of chlorophyll a and phycocyanin [16] and the inhibition of the growth of cells [17]. These findings indicate that it is worth studying ultrasound pretreatment for the treatment of algae-containing water.

However, the promotion of coagulation by sonication is affected by the ultrasonic parameters and characteristics of algal cells. Changes in the ultrasonic frequency have no significant effect on algae removal, but increasing the power and duration can have negative effects on coagulation [18]. Furthermore it is reported that excessive use of ultrasound can cause the release of microcystin [17], as well as the leakage of DON [19], which would affect the safety of drinking water. Therefore, the appropriate setting of the ultrasound parameters is crucial.

It is generally believed that the ideal ultrasound parameters should result in gas vacuole collapse, damage to the membrane/wall of cells (but not lysis to prevent releasing toxins into the water), photosynthesis inhibition, and have high energy efficiency [20]. Previous studies have showen that higher frequencies are more effective for cyanobacterial inhibition than lower frequencies, because higher frequencies are close to the resonance frequency of gas vacuoles, thus making them more prone to collapse [15,21]. However, there are still no recommended parameters settings. Meanwhile, there is still a lack of research on the effects of a series of changes on the coagulation-flocculation after sonication, which is an important step in the application of ultrasound in drinking water plant pretreatment.

In this paper, the parameters setting strategies of ultrasound on algae-laden water treatment are presented from the perspective of enhanced coagulation. A jar test was used to investigate the effect of three important parameters (frequency, power and sonication time) on M. aeruginosa removal in the coagulation process. In addition, the turbidity was selected as the evaluation index, due to its importance in drinking water treatment. Overall, the study specifically focused on (1) the changes in organic matter and physiological structure of algae cells, (2) the contribution of the different mechanisms under ultrasonic conditions, and (3) the parameter setting strategy for ultrasonic treatment application and water plant pretreatment.

M. aeruginosa was selected as a typical species in this study, and was purchased from the Freshwater Algae Culture Collection at the Institute of Hydrobiology. M. aeruginosa was cultured in a light growth incubator (BSG-400, Boxun, China) using BG11 as the growth medium. The incubator conditions were set as 25 ± 1 °C with 12 h light and 12 h darkness. The experiments were performed in the stabilized phase.

Coomassie Brilliant Blue G-250, ethanol, bovine serum albumin (BSA) for protein content determination, glucose for polysaccharide determination, salicylic acid (SA) and its hydroxylated products, 2,3-dihdroxybenzoic acid (2,3-DHBA) and 2,5-dihdroxybenzoic acid (2,5-DHBA) were obtained from Aladdin and were analytical grade. Phosphoric acid, phenol and sulfuric acid were purchased from Chongqing Chuangdong Chemical and were also analytical grade.

A set of high-frequency ultrasound equipment (Fig. 1 ) was customized from the Institute of Acoustics of the Chinese Academy of Sciences. One of the four models of transducers (120 kHz, 430 kHz, 740 kHz and 1.12 MHz) was placed at the bottom of a beaker with a capacity of 2000 mL. Another beaker with a prepared algal suspension was placed on the transducer with cooling water around. The acoustic power was adjustable and was measured by a calorimeter [22].
(1)
I =
d T
d t
C P
M
/ V
where I is the ultrasonic power density (W/ml), T is the temperature of the sonicated liquid (°C), t is the irradiation time (seconds), C p is the heat capacity of water (C p is 4.2 ×
10
3
J/(kg°C) at 25 °C), M is the mass of the sonicated liquid (kg), and V is the volume of the sonicated liquid (mL). The acoustic power range of each transducer was approximately 0.09–0.01 W/mL after measurement and calculation. The acoustic power density was measured without cooling water around.

The cell density of the algae suspension for the jar tests was adjusted to
1 ×
10
6
cell/mL (the absorption at 680 nm was 0.050) using water that had stood for a period of time at room temperature (25 °C) to remove residual chlorine. The initial pH (8.50) was not adjusted. The algae suspensions were sonicated for 1 min using a series of varying powers levels and four frequencies. The algae suspensions were also sonicated at 0.08 and 0.02 W/mL and four frequencies for 0 s, 10 s, 60 s, 300 s, 600 s and 1200 s. One liter of prepared algae suspension after sonication was added into each beaker of the jar tester (ZR4-6, Zhongrun, China). The stirring procedure was as follows: samples were fully mixed at 50 rpm for 2 min. Then, a PAC solution was added to each beaker immediately and samples were mixed at 200 rpm for 2 min and at 50 rpm for 10 min. Finally, the samples were left for 15 min. Samples were taken from 2 cm below the water surface and the main parameter (turbidity) was determined. The concentration of PAC in each beaker was 10 mg/L. Every first sample of each coagulation test is a blank control group. The zeta potential measurement during coagulation after addition of PAC was collected after one minute of rapid stirring.

The cell density of the algae suspension was adjusted to
1 ×
10
7
cell/mL (the absorption at 680 nm was 0.50) to explore the ultrasonic mechanism using ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ cm), which was produced from a water purifier (18.25 MΩ cm, UPH-II-20T, Sichuan Ulpure, China). The prepared algae suspension was also sonicated at 0.08 and 0.02 W/mL and at a typical frequency (740 kHz) for 0 s, 10 s, 30 s, 60 s, 300 s, 600 s and 1200 s.

The cell concentrations of the samples were enumerated using microscope (BA310, Motic, China) and the absorbance at 680 nm was measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (DR5000, HACH, America) to determine the linear relationship. The turbidity was measured using a turbidity meter (1900C, HACH, America). The turbidity removal after coagulation is expressed by the removal rate using the following formula:
(2)
N R
%
=
N 0
-
N i
N 0
× 100
where NR is the turbidity removal rate after coagulation (%),
N i
is the turbidity of the sample (NTU) and
N 0
is the initial turbidity of the sample (NTU). To eliminate the error between different coagulation experiments, we used the improvement of the turbidity removal rate Δ
N R
%
to express the influence of sonication in enhancing coagulation:
(3)
Δ N R
%
=
NR
i
-
NR
1
where
NR
i
is the turbidity removal rate after coagulation (%) of a test group of samples and
NR
1
is the turbidity removal rate after coagulation (%) of the blank control samples.

The composition and content of AOM were measured to evaluate the effects of sonication on algae cells. Extracellular organic matter (EOM) was extracted from the algae suspension by centrifuging at 8000 rpm for 15 min and then filtering the supernatant through a 0.45
μ m
membrane [23]. After that, the algae cells were resuspended. The suspended cells were destroyed by three freezing/unfreezing cycles to release the intracellular organic matter (IOM). Then, the IOM was extracted by centrifuging at 8000 rpm for 15 min and filtering the supernatant through the 0.45
μ m
membrane.

AOM was tested on the content of total soluble proteins (TDP), polysaccharides and fluorescence characteristics. The Bradford method was used to determine the concentration of TDP, with the standard of bovine serum albumin [24]. The phenol-sulfuric acid method was used to determine the concentration of polysaccharides, with the standard of glucose [25]. The excitation-emission matrix (EEM) fluorescence spectra of the samples were determined with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F96Pro, Lengguang Technology, China) with a scanning emission spectrum from 200 to 700 nm at 1 nm increments and an excitation spectrum from 200 to 700 nm at 5 nm increments.

The nanoparticle size and ZETA potential analyzer (Nano ZS90, Malven, Britain) was used to test the zeta potential. The level of dissolved organic carbon (DOC) and specific ultraviolet absorbance (SUVA) of the suspension were measured after filtering through the 0.45
μ m
membrane. UV254 was measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (DR5000, HACH, America) and a TOC analyzer (TOC-L Series, Shimadzu, Japan) was used to determine the level of DOC. The SUVA (L m−1 mg C−1) was calculated as follows:
(4)
S U V A =
U V
254
D O C

The samples were centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 15 min for SEM analysis. After the coagulation process completed, the bottom algae-containing flocs were filtered through a 0.45 μm nylon membrane filter. Collected algae cells/filters were fixed with 2.5% glutaldehyde over night (4 °C).

Using the method of Louise [26], salicylic acid was used in the dosimeter during the indirect measurement of hydroxyl radicals. A high-performance liquid chromatograph (Hitachi Chromaster, Japan) equipped with an autosampler (Hitachi 5210, Japan) and a dual λ absorbance detector (Hitachi 5420, Japan) was used to quantify the concentrations of the salicylic acid, 2,3-DHBA and 2,5-DHBA with the UV detector wavelength setting at 325 nm and a chromatogram run time of 18 min. The column was a Venusil XBP C18 column (5 μm, 4.6 mm × 250 mm, Agela, China). The mobile phase was a mixture of phosphoric acid (0.02 M; pH 2.5) and methanol at a ratio of 60:40 with a flow rate of 1.0 mL/min. A column temperature of 30 °C and an injection volume of 20 μL were set.

Fig. 2 shows the influence of the frequency and power on algae removal. To understand the trend of the data, in all of the calculations, the results of the control group were subtracted from the results of the test groups (
Δ N R
%
). When the acoustic power density in the ultrasonic irradiation lasting 60 s was increased, the
Δ N R
increased. When the ultrasound frequency and the acoustic power density were 740 kHz and 0.09 W/mL, respectively, the turbidity removal rate of the algae suspension increased from approximately 83–91% after sonication and coagulation (Fig. S1). The reason may be that in the short duration of sonication, the increase in the acoustic power density resulted in intense physical and chemical effects, causing a small amount of organic matters release that may have promoted coagulation.

Variance analysis shows that both the frequency and power significantly affected the turbidity removal rate (p < 0.05) (Table S1), while the power played a dominant role (p < 0.05) between these two factors, which was confirmed by the simple effect analysis (Text S1, Text S2). For instance, when the acoustic power density was set at 0.09 W/mL, the removal rate differences at the four frequencies were not obvious (p > 0.05), but when the acoustic power density was reduced to 0.01 W/mL, there were obvious differences between the removal rate at 120 kHz and that at 1.12 MHz (p < 0.05). These result were similar to the results of Liang [18].

Generally, higher acoustic powers were required to induce cavitation at high frequencies [27]. And the collapse of cavitation bubbles causes both mechanical and chemical effects, which can lead to the release of cell content and even the lysis of cell walls [28]. The mechanical effect decreases as the ultrasonic frequency increases [29,30]. As for chemical effects, the increase of frequency results in decreases both in the size and the temperature of cavitation bubbles, which cause a decrease in the amount of radicals per bubble, while it also results in an increase in the number of cavitation bubbles [31]. Therefore, there is a threshold range of frequency where the maximum number of radicals are generated. Study [32] indicates that there is no significant difference in the H2O2 yields at 213, 355, 647 kHz under roughly the same power level, while the H2O2 yield at 1056 kHz is the lowest (at the acoustic power density range of 0.0125 W/mL to approximately 0.1 W/mL).

Some studies reported that for the disruption of algal cells, chemical effects may not the main mechanism [33]. However, for the process of coagulation, the chemical effects of the high active radicals may not be ignored because they can induce the changes in the characteristics of cell membranes and extracellular substances. The complex effect mechanism of ultrasound usage caused a nonlinear response between the effects and the frequency-power variations, which caused varying degrees of cells damage and changes of extracellular substances characteristics. Therefore, the changes of cell morphological and substances characteristics were chosen to analyze the mechanism of ultrasound-enhanced coagulation.

The sonication time is another important parameter in sonication-enhanced coagulation. Two specific settings of the acoustic power density (0.02 W/mL and 0.08 W/mL) were selected to determine the effects of the sonication time. During the initial several minutes, frequency of 120 kHz and 430 kHz at a lower power density level (0.02 W/mL) showed an increasing turbidity removal and a decreasing removal, with even a negative impact following a longer duration of radiation (Fig. 3 (a), (b)), but frequencies of 740 kHz and 1.12 MHz showed a positive impact (Fig. 3 (c) (d)). Compared with the higher frequencies, the lower frequencies caused cavitation easier, and the increased duration enhanced these sonochemical effects [20]. Thus, at lower frequencies and power density levels but for a longer treatment time, the sonochemical effects strongly increased the damage of the algae cells and induced the release of IOM, which disturbed coagulation.

In comparison, the results were different when the acoustic power density was set at 0.08 W/mL. As Fig. 3 shows, there is a continuous reduction in turbidity removal and a negative impact on coagulation with increasing irradiation duration. Compared with 0.02 W/mL, the results at a level of 0.08 W/mL indicated intense sonochemical and mechanical effects at the same frequency, which caused more severe damage to the cells.

The increase in the sonication time may have reinforced the different effects produced at various frequency and power levels, further causing changes in the algae cells and related compounds, which had a significant effect on the coagulation effect. This mechanism will be discussed in Section 3.3.

According to the results of the previous section, a duration of 5 min was chosen as the demarcation point of the beginning of the negative effects of using ultrasound. To observe the changes in the cell’s morphology, especially the integrity of the cells, a higher acoustic power density of 0.09 W/mL was selected.

Fig. 4 shows the cells’ morphological changes after sonication at different frequency. SEM micrographs showed obvious morphological damage and dispersibility of cells (Fig. 4 (e) (g)) after sonication at 430 and 740 kHz, and with an acoustic power density of 0.09 W/mL for 5 min compared with the original smooth cell mass (Fig. 4 (a)). At the same time, folds on the cell surface became deeper and the cell wall showed significant damage ((Fig. 4 (b) (f) (h)). However, for 120 and 1120 kHz, although the ultrasonic treatment also caused damage to the cells, it was not as serious as 430 and 740 kHz ((Fig. 4 (c) (d) (i) (j)).

Sonication at 0.09 W/mL for 5 min did not cause a large amount of cell rupture. However, damage to the cell membrane was clearly observed due to lipid peroxidation on the cell membrane caused by free radicals. Many studies have found an increase in malondialdehyde (MDA) [15], which is characteristic of the peroxidation reaction of lipids during sonication. Moreover, the more obvious cellular damage showed by 430 and 740 kHz also illustrated the significant effects of chemical effects, because radicals have a greater amount of production at this frequency as described in Section 3.1.1. The study of Xiaoge [28] also showed similar results.

The trends of the instantaneous turbidity of the suspension after sonication are very similar for every condition, from 12.41 ± 0.56 NTU to 5.77 ± 0.39 NTU (Fig. 5) in a short time. However, the SEM micrographs showed a negligible number of lysed cells in Fig. 4. Therefore, the decrease in turbidity may be caused by the following two reasons. On the one hand, this may be due to the mechanical effects of the ultrasound scattering the aggregated cell mass thereby dispersing individual cells. However, since the turbidity meter detects the scattering intensity in the 90°-direction of light, the effect of particle size is slight. On the other hand, the decrease in turbidity may be due to the collapse of the gas vesicles. Because the inflatable space has a lower refractive index than the surrounding aqueous medium, the collapse of the gas vesicles in suspension resulted in reduced turbidity [34].

Many previous studies have attributed the collapse of gas vesicles in general to resonance effects [20,21], and the resonance frequency was estimated by the following equation [35]:
(4)
f =
1
2 π a
3 γ
ρ
p 0
+
2 σ
a
-
2 σ
a ρ
where f is the frequency, a is the radius of the bubble, γ is the ratio of the heat capacities of the gas,
ρ
is the density of the surrounding medium,
p 0
is the ambient pressure and
σ
is thesurface tension of the surrounding medium. In reference to the calculation method of Zhang et al. [21], for air, γ is 1.39,
p 0
is 105 Pa,
ρ
is 1.0 g/cm3 and
σ
is ignored. Thus, the theoretical radii of the resonant bubbles at the four frequencies (120 kHz, 430 kHz, 740 kHz and 1.12 MHz) used in the experiment are approximately 0.026 mm, 7.3 μm, 4.3 μm and 2.8 μm, respectively. Although there is a difference in the size of the gas vesicles in cells according to different studies: for a radius of 3–5 μm [15] and a diameter of 1 μm [36], the theoretical resonance frequency calculated according to the formula above is 1.3–2.16 MHz [15] and 6.5 MHz [36], respectively.

According to this theory, only a frequency of 1.12 MHz may achieve the resonance effect in this experiment. However, Fig. 5 shows that the change in instantaneous turbidity is not strongly related with the frequency.

It was reported that a pressure of 0.6 MPa caused all gas vesicles of A. ovalisporum to collapse and the turbidity decreased by 80%. The apparent mean critical collapse pressure of gas vesicles was 0.27 ± 0.02 MPa (cells suspended in a medium) [34]. Similarly, ultrasonic cavitation also caused local high temperatures and high pressures. Merouani [37] confirmed that the pressure suddenly increased to approximately 140 MPa inside the bubble at the end of the collapse of the bubble by simulation. Therefore, the mechanism that caused turbidity reduction and gas vesicle collapse may be the enormous pressure generated by the ultrasonic cavitation effect rather than the resonance effect, which requires further experimental proof. Thus, turbidity changes that are very similar after sonication using different frequencies must be well explained. At the same time, because the power density affected the mechanical effects of the ultrasound, the turbidity at the lower power density in Fig. 5 decreased slower than the turbidity at the higher power density.

Therefore, it is pointless to search for a resonance frequency of the gas vesicles. The reason why the effects of algae removal were different at different frequencies was due more to the different mechanical and chemical effects when using different frequencies and the same power density and duration conditions. Thus, we selected a specific frequency (740 kHz), which showed better algae removal than the others frequencies, to perform the following study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Composition and content changes of AOM

ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the effects of sonication on AOM release and degradation, the effects of sonication at 740 kHz and a power density level of 0.08 W/mL on the fluorescence EEM spectra of AOM were studied (Fig. 6 ). There were three typical peaks: T, C and D. Peak T, which appeared at excitation-emission wavelengths of 260–300 nm/300–380 nm, was related to protein-like region of substances, which corresponded closely with tryptophan [38]. Peak C, which appeared at Ex-Em wavelengths of 300–370 nm/380–480 nm, represented humic-like substances [38]. Peak D, which appeared at Ex-Em wavelengths of approximately 600 nm/650 nm and only in IOM, may have represented phycocyanin because the peak of phycocyanin is strong at excitation wavelengths of 550–650 nm and at an emission wavelength of approximately 645 nm [39,40].

The results showed that with increasing sonication time, protein-like substances (peak T) were distinctly increasing and humic-like substances (peak C) inconspicuously decreased in EOM (Fig. 6 (a) (c) (e)). In IOM, by contrast, protein-like substances (peak T) were more abundant than in EOM and significantly decreased (Fig. 6 (b’) (d’) (f ’)). Similarly, phycocyanin (in IOM) also showed a decrease (Fig. 6 (b) (d) (f)). Previous studies reached similar conclusions that protein-like substances and phycocyanin fluorescence decreased up to 90% after exposure to ozone, chlorine dioxide and free chlorine [40], while phycocyanin decreased 44.8% when sonicated for 5 min at 0.32 W/mL [41]. In contrast, there was no obvious peak of phycocyanin in EOM, and the amplitude of the increase of peak T in EOM was lower than that in IOM, indicating that the IOM was not directly released after the ultrasonic radiation, but participated in a series of reactions such as scavenging the accumulated ROS due to environmental stresses [42].

Several excellent studies have focused on the composition and characterization of EOM and it mainly consisted of hydrophilic polysaccharides and hydrophobic proteins [43]. Charged polysaccharides dominated the charge density [44] and proteins governed the hydrophobicity [43]. In this research, we studied the change in total soluble proteins and polysaccharides in EOM after sonication at a typical frequency (740 kHz) and different acoustic power densities (0.08 W/mL and 0.02 W/mL). As shown in Fig. 7
, the polysaccharide content was higher than the protein content in EOM. As the sonication time increased, the protein content increased significantly, and the increase in the acoustic power density also increased the protein content. This finding is consistent with the observations from the EEM fluorescence spectra of EOM. By contrast, there was no clear trend in the content of polysaccharides. Combined with Fig. 3(c), which showed a significant change in turbidity removal, the results indicated that the increased protein played a more important role in coagulation efficiency by sonication than the polysaccharides, which remained unchanged.

EOM mainly affects coagulation by the following aspects: 1) EOM controls the surface charge of cells, which controls the electronegative nature of the zeta potential of algal cells in natural water. The pH is the dissociation of functional groups such as carboxylic acid groups [8,45], which comes from hydrophilic charged polysaccharides and protein. 2) EOM can chelate metal cations and increase the dosage of coagulant. Research has shown that a model of EOM, alginate, can combine with aluminum ions [46]. Meanwhile, the protein in EOM also has the capacity of complexing with the coagulant [47]. Thus, the release of protein has negative effects on coagulation. However, studies have shown that sonication can induce the cross-linking of protein molecules forming high molecular weight proteins [48]. In summary, EOM, especially protein, affects coagulation by a variety of mechanisms, and Tirado-Miranda confirmed that bridging flocculation is the main mechanism at a low degree of protein surface coverage, but the electric charge is significant at a high degree of coverage [49]. Notably, there was an obvious improvement after a few minutes of sonication in the jar test (Fig. 3(c)). Thus, in the process of sonication-enhanced coagulation to remove algae, the effects of AOM could be interpreted as the ultrasound inducing the release of IOM when the suspension was irradiated for a short time (approximately 5 min). EOM was at a low concentration, and the coagulation was enhanced by adhesion bridging. If the suspension had been irradiated for a longer time, the negative charge on the cell surface would have increased, and the significant competition of flocculants would have restrained coagulation.

Fig. 8 shows that in the process of sonication, the absolute value of the zeta potential decreased in the first few minutes and towards the direction of facilitating flocculation and tended to stabilize in the long-term. However, other pre-oxidation methods caused the increase of absolute values of zeta potential to different degree such as KMnO4 [50] and UV/H2O2 [51]. Meanwhile, pretreatment with MnO2 showed a strong adsorption capacity, and the absolute value of the zeta potential decreased [52]. Therefore, the ultrasonic treatment showed an effect different from that of pure pre-oxidation, which may be due to its mechanical effect similar to MnO2.

Therefore, a similar conclusion was made that for M. aeruginosa, when sonicated for a short time (approximately 5 min), the zeta potential changed to improve coagulation, and the release of protein was minimal. However, during the longer lasting sonication, the zeta potential tended to be stable and enough protein was release to obstruct coagulation and threaten the safety of water quality because it can contribute to high DON, which is the main precursor of disinfection byproducts [19].

In addition, tests on the DOC and SUVA also showed similar results. Table 1 shows that at 0.02 W/mL, sonication on algal suspension decreased the DOC at first, but with increasing time, the release and degradation of intracellular matter caused a small range of fluctuations in the DOC. However, at 0.08 W/mL, the DOC continued to rise after a significant initial decrease. Combined with the previous analysis, this result could be due to the massive release of intracellular substances when high-intensity ultrasound is used.

All the data showed small fluctuations during the 5 min, and when the sonication time was increased to 20 min, both of the SUVA values increased significantly. Most of the current studies show that a low SUVA value has a good correlation with the high hydrophilicity of organic matter [43,53]. Thus, the increase in SUVA was due to the release of hydrophobic protein.

To evaluate the sonochemical and mechanical effects, the generation of hydroxyl radicals was determined. As Table 2 shows, the amount of hydroxyl radicals increased with sonication time, and the amount of hydroxyl radicals at a high power level was much greater than that at a lower power level. Moreover, the lower power required a more time to generate the free radicals. Compared with the results of sonication-enhanced coagulation (Fig. 3), the turbidity removal rate in the presence of free radicals dropped significantly. This may be explained by the accumulation of released IOM as mentioned above, or it may also be due to the production of hydroxyl radicals that caused greater damage to the cells. However, it has been proven that the addition of a radical scavenger did not affect the inactivation efficiency of the algae cells [21]. At the same time, it was further confirmed that the generation of hydroxyl radicals did not cause a significant change in the instantaneous turbidity (Fig. 5).

This may be because both phycocyanin and extracellular polysaccharides have antioxidant activity [54,55] and can protect cells to some extent. However, the generated free radicals may react with extracellular substances to affect coagulation. Combined with 3.2 and 3.3, although the large amount of hydroxyl radical production significantly affected the morphology of cells to improve coagulation, it also caused a large release of IOM to inhibit coagulation. Thus, the chemical effects should be controlled to avoid large releases of IOM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Changes of zeta potential and flocs after coagulation
ce:section.ce:para: For the zeta potential in the coagulation process, Fig. 9 shows that there were no significant differences at these four frequencies in a low-level of acoustic power density (0.02 W/mL) after the addition of PAC. This means that the frequency had little effect on the Zeta potential during the coagulation process. Similar to the trend without adding PAC (Fig. 8), short-time ultrasonic pretreatment caused a decrease in the absolute value of zeta potential during coagulation (from approximately −20 mV to −15 mV), and tended to stabilize in the long-term. At the same time, the small amplitude changes of zeta potential also indicated that the main mechanism of ultrasonic enhanced coagulation was bridging rather than charge neutralization.

In order to explore the effect of ultrasonic pretreatment on coagulation of algae-laden water, the changes of algae flocs after coagulation were studied. There are obvious differences between these two kinds of flocs. As shown in Fig. 10 , the algae cells after sonication (Fig. 10 (c) (d)) bound more closely to the flocs than cells without sonication (Fig. 10 (a) (b)) after coagulation. Although there was no obvious damage and lysis of algal cells, the algae cells were surrounded by more dense flocs (Fig. 10 (c) (d)). Meanwhile, more cells aggregated into clumps and larger flocs were formed. This further confirmed the conclusion above that short-duration and low-acoustic power sonication caused a small amount of IOM release, especially the proteins, which enhanced the bridging flocculation between algae cells and high-molecular flocculants and improved the coagulation.

In this study, the effects and mechanisms of ultrasonic parameters on enhancing coagulation were discussed with turbidity as the main indicator. The following conclusions were obtained.
4.1
The appropriate ultrasonic parameters improved the removal of turbidity, which was one of the most significant indexes in drinking water treatment, from approximately 80% to more than 90%. Compared with the acoustic power density and the sonication time, the frequency affected the removal slightly.
4.2
The release of protein after sonication had a great effect on coagulation. A small amount of protein could act as a flocculant; however, as the protein accumulated, it increased the extracellular negative charge and inhibited flocculation.
4.3
The morphological damage of cells after sonication came from two aspects. On the one hand, ultrasound broke up the aggregated cells and damaged the cell surface. On the other hand, the gas vesicle inside the cell collapsed and affected its suspension, which may have been due to the instantaneous high pressure of the rupture of cavitation bubbles.
4.4
Both mechanical and chemical effects had an effect on coagulation, and excessive free radical production had a significant adverse effect on coagulation. To reduce the threat of protein leakage and taking into account that in the long-time and high-levels of the acoustic power indensity would increase power consumption, it was recommended that the application of sonication-enhanced coagulation should be controlled by setting the acoustic power density to 0.02 W/mL and the time to approximately 5 min, which was in the proper mechanical effects and less chemical effects.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51778082), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2017CDJZR) and Social Work and People’s Livelihood Security Science and Technology Innovation Special Project of Chongqing (No. cstc2017shmsA20019).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ultsonch.2019.01.022.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
